---
pagetitle: "Emmanuel Pilliat"
page-layout: full
toc: false
sidebar: false
---

```{=html}
<section class="hero-aurora">
  <canvas class="hero-aurora__lines" id="biisoCanvas"></canvas>
  <div class="hero-aurora__overlay"></div>
  <div class="hero-aurora__inner">
    <img class="hero-aurora__avatar" src="images/profile.jpg" alt="Emmanuel Pilliat">
    <div class="hero-aurora__content">
      <p class="hero-aurora__eyebrow">Statistics · Machine Learning · HPC</p>
      <h1 class="hero-aurora__title">Emmanuel Pilliat</h1>
      <p class="hero-aurora__subtitle">
        Assistant professor in statistics at <strong>ENSAI</strong> (Rennes), working on
        <strong>high-dimensional statistics</strong>, <strong>machine learning</strong>,
        and <strong>high-performance computing</strong> with Julia.
      </p>
      <div class="hero-aurora__links">
        <a class="hero-link" href="https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=H3gN1KgAAAAJ&amp;hl=fr"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> Google Scholar</a>
        <a class="hero-link" href="https://arxiv.org/search/?searchtype=author&amp;query=Pilliat,+E"><i class="bi bi-file-earmark-text"></i> arXiv</a>
        <a class="hero-link" href="https://hal.science/search/index/?q=*&amp;authFullName_s=Emmanuel+Pilliat"><i class="bi bi-archive"></i> HAL</a>
        <a class="hero-link" href="https://github.com/epilliat"><i class="bi bi-github"></i> GitHub</a>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<script>
// Biisotonic matrix background — a JS port of biiso/isogen.py (dyadic generator).
// Each matrix row is a non-decreasing line in [0,1]; we stack N of them as thin white curves.
(function () {
  const canvas = document.getElementById("biisoCanvas");
  if (!canvas) return;
  const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d");
  const N = 140, D = 150;           // stacked lines (rows) × discretisation (columns)
  let M = null;

  function* dyadic(l, r) {
    if (r - l <= 1) return;
    if (r - l === 2) { yield [l, l + 1, r]; return; }
    const med = (l + r) >> 1;
    yield [l, med, r];
    yield* dyadic(l, med);
    yield* dyadic(med, r);
  }

  function generate(n, d) {
    const R = n + 2, C = d + 2;
    const m = Array.from({ length: R }, () => new Float64Array(C).fill(0.5));
    for (let j = 0; j < C; j++) { m[0][j] = 0; m[n + 1][j] = 1; }   // top row 0, bottom row 1
    for (let i = 0; i < R; i++) { m[i][0] = 0; m[i][d + 1] = 1; }   // left col 0, right col 1
    m[0][0] = 0; m[0][d + 1] = 0; m[n + 1][0] = 0; m[n + 1][d + 1] = 1;  // corners
    const rows = [...dyadic(0, n + 1)], cols = [...dyadic(0, d + 1)];
    for (const [down, em, up] of rows) {
      for (const [left, qm, right] of cols) {
        const upCtr = Math.min(m[up][qm], m[em][right]);     // monotone in rows & columns
        const downCtr = Math.max(m[down][qm], m[em][left]);
        m[em][qm] = downCtr + (upCtr - downCtr) * Math.random();
      }
    }
    return m;
  }

  function draw() {
    const dpr = window.devicePixelRatio || 1;
    const w = canvas.offsetWidth, h = canvas.offsetHeight;
    if (!w || !h) return;
    canvas.width = w * dpr; canvas.height = h * dpr;
    ctx.setTransform(dpr, 0, 0, dpr, 0, 0);
    ctx.clearRect(0, 0, w, h);
    ctx.lineWidth = 1; ctx.lineJoin = "round"; ctx.lineCap = "round";
    ctx.strokeStyle = "rgba(255,255,255,0.15)";
    for (let i = 1; i <= N; i++) {
      ctx.beginPath();
      for (let j = 1; j <= D; j++) {
        const x = ((j - 1) / (D - 1)) * w;
        const y = h * (1 - M[i][j]);
        j === 1 ? ctx.moveTo(x, y) : ctx.lineTo(x, y);
      }
      ctx.stroke();
    }
  }

  M = generate(N, D);
  draw();
  let t;
  window.addEventListener("resize", () => { clearTimeout(t); t = setTimeout(draw, 120); });
})();
</script>
```

::: {.aurora-wrap}

I am an assistant professor in statistics at **ENSAI** (Rennes), working on topics related to machine learning and high-dimensional statistics. My PhD centered on two areas in modern statistics: **change-point detection** and **ranking problems**.

I am also interested in **high-performance computing with Julia**, with a focus on GPU architectures. I'm developing [KernelForge.jl](https://epilliat.github.io/KernelForge.jl/dev), a Julia package for portable, high-performance parallel primitives like `mapreduce` and `accumulate`, building on [KernelIntrinsics.jl](https://epilliat.github.io/KernelIntrinsics.jl).

**Contact:** firstname.lastname@ensai.fr

```{=html}
<div class="aurora-card-grid">
  <a class="aurora-card" href="research.html">
    <span class="aurora-card__icon">📄</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__title">Research</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__desc">Preprints &amp; publications in COLT, Annals of Statistics, SODA, TMLR, EJS.</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__more">View research →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="aurora-card" href="software/software.html">
    <span class="aurora-card__icon">⚙️</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__title">Software</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__desc">KernelForge.jl &amp; KernelIntrinsics.jl — portable GPU/CPU primitives in Julia.</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__more">View software →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="aurora-card" href="teaching/teaching.html">
    <span class="aurora-card__icon">🎓</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__title">Teaching</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__desc">Hypothesis testing, (G)LM and parallel computing at ENSAI Rennes.</span>
    <span class="aurora-card__more">View teaching →</span>
  </a>
</div>
```

:::
